perturbation.original_text: 4.5 Late Payments: In the event that any payment due hereunder is not made when due, the payment shall accrue interest beginning on the tenth day following the due date thereof, calculated at the annual rate of six percent (6%), the interest being compounded on the last day of each calendar year. Each such royalty payment when made shall be accompanied by all interest so accrued. Said interest and the payment and acceptance thereof shall not negate or waive the right of JHU to seek any other remedy, legal or equitable, to which it may be entitled because of the delinquency of any payment.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.5 Late Payments: In the event that any payment due hereunder is not made when due, the payment shall accrue interest beginning on the ninetieth day following the due date thereof, calculated at the annual rate of twenty-five percent (25%), the interest being compounded on the last day of each calendar year. Each such royalty payment when made shall be accompanied by all interest so accrued. Said interest and the payment and acceptance thereof shall not negate or waive the right of JHU to seek any other remedy, legal or equitable, to which it may be entitled because of the delinquency of any payment.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the late payment terms to accrue interest after 90 days at a rate of 25% introduces a legal contradiction as it may violate usury laws, which regulate the maximum interest rate that can be charged on a debt. Many states have usury laws that set limits on interest rates, and a rate of 25% could exceed those limits, especially for commercial transactions.
perturbation.law_explanation: Maryland law sets the legal rate of interest at 6% and provides a maximum rate of 8% in most cases, while this change would impose a rate of 25%.

(a) (1) Except as provided in subsections (b), (c), (d), (e), and (f) of this section, a lender may charge interest at an effective rate of simple interest not in excess of 8 percent per year on the unpaid principal balance of a loan if there is a written agreement signed by the borrower which sets forth the stated rate of interest charged by the lender. (2) If a loan made under paragraph (1) of this subsection is secured by the pledge of collateral which is a certificate of deposit held by the borrower, the lender may charge interest at a rate not to exceed 2 percent in excess of the rate of interest payable on the certificate of deposit. (3) If a loan made under paragraph (1) of this subsection is secured by the pledge of collateral which is other than a savings account or if such loan is unsecured, the lender may charge a rate of interest not in excess of 18 percent. However, on a loan made on or after July 1, 1982, a lender may charge an effective rate of simple interest not in excess of 24 percent per year on the unpaid principal balance, provided that: (i) If the loan is a renewal or refinancing of a loan made prior to July 1, 1982, the lender complies with § 12–116 of this subtitle; (ii) If the loan includes a provision for a rate of interest which may be adjusted by the lender during the term of the loan, the lender complies with § 12–118 of this subtitle; (iii) Upon the borrower’s default, if the loan is secured by personal property, the lender complies with § 12–115 of this subtitle concerning repossession and redemption of the goods securing the loan; (iv) If the loan is for the purchase of consumer goods, the loan contract complies with § 12–117 of this subtitle; and (v) The loan does not include a balloon payment, unless payment in full is due on demand or in one year or less. (b) (1) A lender may charge interest at any effective rate of simple interest on the unpaid principal balance of a loan if: (i) There is a written agreement signed by the borrow
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the late payment terms to accrue interest after 90 days at a rate of 25%. The Law Explanation states that Maryland law sets a legal interest rate at 6% and a maximum rate of 8% in most cases. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this, indicating that a lender may charge interest at an effective rate not exceeding 8% per year unless specific conditions are met (which are not evidently applicable here). Charging 25% interest directly contradicts these statutory limits, making the changed text non-compliant and creating a significant legal contradiction by violating usury laws.

perturbation.original_text: 6.2 Termination by Company: Company may terminate this Agreement and the license granted herein, for any reason, upon giving JHU sixty (60) days written notice under Paragraph 8.1.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.2 Termination by Company: Company may terminate this Agreement and the license granted herein, for any reason, upon giving JHU fifteen (15) days written notice under Paragraph 8.1.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period from 60 days to 15 days could be considered unreasonable depending on the industry standards and the complexity of the licensed technology. Such a short notice period may violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, which is part of contract law in most US states, including Maryland. A court could find that 15 days is insufficient time for JHU to find another licensee or otherwise mitigate its damages.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Although there is no explicit statute, Maryland courts recognize the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. 15 days notice to terminate the contract, especially given the nature of the agreement, could be seen as an unreasonable time and a violation of this covenant.

perturbation.justification: The change from 60 days to 15 days notice for termination introduces a potential legal contradiction under the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, which exists in Maryland contract law. The Law Explanation confirms that Maryland courts recognize this covenant. Although there are no specific statutes in the Scraped Law Snippets, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both strongly suggest that 15 days could be deemed unreasonable, particularly considering the nature of the agreement, thus potentially violating this implied covenant. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition where a time-sensitive obligation is modified in a way that violates state law (the implied covenant).

perturbation.original_text: 4.6 Records: The Company shall make and retain, for a period of three (3) years following the period of each report required by Paragraph 4.4, true and accurate records, files and books of account containing all the data reasonably required for the full computation and verification of sales and other information required in Paragraph 4.4. Such books and records shall be in accordance with generally accepted accounting principles consistently applied. The Company shall permit the inspection and copying of such records, files and books of account by JHU or its agents during regular business hours upon ten (10) business days' written notice to the Company. Such inspection shall not be made more than once each calendar year. All costs of such inspection and copying shall be paid by JHU, provided that if any such inspection shall reveal that an error has been made in the amount equal to five percent (5%) or more of such payment, such costs shall be borne by the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.6 Records: The Company shall make and retain, for a period of one (1) year following the period of each report required by Paragraph 4.4, true and accurate records, files and books of account containing all the data reasonably required for the full computation and verification of sales and other information required in Paragraph 4.4. Such books and records shall be in accordance with generally accepted accounting principles consistently applied. The Company shall permit the inspection and copying of such records, files and books of account by JHU or its agents during regular business hours upon ten (10) business days' written notice to the Company. Such inspection shall not be made more than once each calendar year. All costs of such inspection and copying shall be paid by JHU, provided that if any such inspection shall reveal that an error has been made in the amount equal to five percent (5%) or more of such payment, such costs shall be borne by the Company.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the record retention period to one year may conflict with IRS regulations and generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP), which often recommend or require longer retention periods, especially when dealing with financial transactions that could be subject to audit or legal inquiry, this is especially the case given the nature of the company's operations and the existing agreement terms. Depending on the specifics of the company's tax situation and the nature of the licensed products, a one-year retention period could prevent JHU from being able to verify records in case of a dispute, especially since legal action can occur after this one-year retention period.
perturbation.law_explanation: 26 CFR § 1.6001-1 mandates that records be kept as long as they may be material in the administration of any internal revenue law. Given potential audits or disputes over royalties, a one-year retention period is highly unlikely to comply with the spirit and intent of federal tax law regarding recordkeeping.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 26 CFR § 1.6001-1 - Records. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 26 CFR § 1.6001-1 - Records. CFR prev | next § 1.6001-1 Records. (a) In general. Except as provided in paragraph (b) of this section, any person subject to tax under subtitle A of the Code (including a qualified State individual income tax which is treated pursuant to section 6361(a) as if it were imposed by chapter 1 of subtitle A), or any person required to file a return of information with respect to income , shall keep such permanent books of account or records, including inventories , as are sufficient to establish the amount of gross income , deductions , credits, or other matters required to be shown by such person in any return of such tax or information. (b) Farmers and wage-earners. Individuals deriving gross income from the business of farming, and individuals whose gross income includes salaries, wages , or similar compensation for personal services rendered, are required with respect to such income to keep such records as will enable the district director to determine the correct amount of income subject to the tax. It is not necessary, however, that with respect to such income individuals keep the books of account or records required by paragraph (a) of this section. For rules with respect to the records to be kept in substantiation of traveling and other business expenses of employees , see § 1.162-17. (c) Exempt organizations. In addition to such permanent books and records as are required by paragraph (a) of this section with respect to the tax imposed by section 511 on unrelated business income of certain exempt organizations , every organization exempt from tax under section 501(a) shall keep such permanent books of account or records, including inventories , as are sufficient to show specifically the items of gross income , receipts and disbursements. Such organizations shall also keep such bo
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the record retention period from three years to one year. The law explanation states that 26 CFR § 1.6001-1 requires records to be kept as long as they may be material in the administration of any internal revenue law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 26 CFR § 1.6001-1, states that persons subject to tax must keep permanent books of account or records sufficient to establish the amount of gross income, deductions, credits, or other matters required to be shown in any tax return. The perturbation explanation indicates a one-year retention period may not comply with GAAP and could prevent JHU from verifying records in case of a dispute. Therefore, the change directly contradicts the law's intention to ensure records are available for tax administration by shortening the retention period.
